question: A 43-year-old man has had hematuria and intermittent abdominal pain for two years. Cross-sectional imaging was obtained; based on the image, which diagnosis is most likely?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the coronal CT image for lesion location and internal characteristics. I see a large, predominantly midline, supravesical mass that appears to arise from the anterosuperior aspect of the bladder and extends upward toward the umbilical region. The lesion is largely cystic in attenuation with a denser, nodular solid component at its inferior (caudal) pole that enhances with contrast; there are curvilinear peripheral calcifications along the wall. Small adjacent lymph nodes are visible in the paravesical and para-aortic regions.

Next, I relate these imaging findings to the question asking which diagnosis is most likely. The combination of a midline, supravesical mass in continuity with the bladder, with both cystic and enhancing solid mural components plus wall calcification and regional nodes, points to a lesion originating in the urachal region rather than a purely intravesical or mesenteric process.

Then I consider how each option fits the observed features. A primary urothelial bladder tumor would more typically be an intraluminal or bladder-wall mass centered within the bladder rather than a midline supravesical mass extending toward the umbilicus. An infected urachal cyst could be cystic, but the presence of an enhancing solid mural nodule and calcification with nodal enlargement favors neoplasm over simple infection. A mesenteric cyst or an extragonadal teratoma would be expected to lie within the mesentery or retroperitoneum and not to arise from the anterosuperior bladder with this direct supravesical extension.

Therefore, based on the lesion’s anatomic origin at the bladder dome/anterosuperior wall, its supravesical extension toward the umbilicus, mixed cystic and solid enhancing components, and peripheral calcification with nodal enlargement, the most likely diagnosis is urachal adenocarcinoma.
caption: Coronal CT image depicting a large and heterogeneous median abdominal mass, extending from the anterosuperior aspect of the bladder towards the umbilicus.
sub_caption: Coronal CT image depicting a large and heterogeneous median abdominal mass, extending from the anterosuperior aspect of the bladder towards the umbilicus.
image_context: A 43-year-old man complained of haematuria and spastic abdominal pain for two years to a general practitioner. Ignoring the symptoms and attributing them to kidney stone disease until a severe lumbar pain attack that had lasted for about 24 hours when he decided to search for medical help. An ultrasonography of the abdomen detected a cystic abdominal mass. Later he was hospitalized in the “Second Surgery“ department of UMHAT „St. George“ Plovdiv, Bulgaria where a second abdominal ultrasonography confirmed a 13 cm large pelvic tumor. A contrast-enchanced CT revealed a tumor formation originating from the upper-anterior wall of the urinary bladder and growing cranio-medially to the abdominal cavity, right below the anterior abdominal wall. The shape of the formation was oval, elongated, and with sharp contours. The dimensions were: longitudinal - 129 mm, transverse - 67 mm, antero-posterior - 66 mm. [<xref rid="gr1" ref-type="fig">Fig. 1</xref>]. The formation was with a cystic character, shell-like calcifications on the periphery were present, a calcium-dense structure in the interior was described and a solid nodular part, absorbing the contrast substance located in the region of the caudal pole. The density of the cyst fluid was about 25 HU, remaining unchanged to contrast. The surrounding adipose tissue was intact. As an additional finding a paravesical lymph node on the right with a size of 7.5 mm, a para-aortic lymph node −7.5 mm, several mesenteric lymph nodes with sizes up to 6 mm and pyelonehritis of the left kidney were described.]. The formation was with a cystic character, shell-like calcifications on the periphery were present, a calcium-dense structure in the interior was described and a solid nodular part, absorbing the contrast substance located in the region of the caudal pole. The density of the cyst fluid was about 25 HU, remaining unchanged to contrast. The surrounding adipose tissue was intact. As an additional finding a paravesical lymph node on the right with a size of 7.5 mm, a para-aortic lymph node −7.5 mm, several mesenteric lymph nodes with sizes up to 6 mm and pyelonehritis of the left kidney were described.Fig. 1Coronal CT image depicting a large and heterogeneous median abdominal mass, extending from the anterosuperior aspect of the bladder towards the umbilicus.Fig. 1
relevant_image_context: A 43-year-old man complained of haematuria and spastic abdominal pain for two years to a general practitioner. Ignoring the symptoms and attributing them to kidney stone disease until a severe lumbar pain attack that had lasted for about 24 hours when he decided to search for medical help. An ultrasonography of the abdomen detected a cystic abdominal mass. Later he was hospitalized in the “Second Surgery“ department of UMHAT „St. George“ Plovdiv, Bulgaria where a second abdominal ultrasonography confirmed a 13 cm large pelvic tumor. A contrast-enhanced CT revealed a tumor formation originating from the upper-anterior wall of the urinary bladder and growing cranio-medially to the abdominal cavity, right below the anterior abdominal wall. The shape of the formation was oval, elongated, and with sharp contours. The dimensions were: longitudinal - 129 mm, transverse - 67 mm, antero-posterior - 66 mm. The formation was with a cystic character, shell-like calcifications on the periphery were present, a calcium-dense structure in the interior was described and a solid nodular part, absorbing the contrast substance located in the region of the caudal pole. The density of the cyst fluid was about 25 HU, remaining unchanged to contrast. The surrounding adipose tissue was intact. As an additional finding a paravesical lymph node on the right with a size of 7.5 mm, a para-aortic lymph node −7.5 mm, several mesenteric lymph nodes with sizes up to 6 mm and pyelonehritis of the left kidney were described. Fig. 1Coronal CT image depicting a large and heterogeneous median abdominal mass, extending from the anterosuperior aspect of the bladder towards the umbilicus.
